title: How IndiGo and Air India Are Reshaping India's Long-Haul Map
dek: India's two largest carriers are pushing aggressively into wide-body long-haul flying, redrawing the country's international network around Delhi, Mumbai and Bengaluru.
body:
For years India's enormous outbound travel market was served largely by foreign hubs, with passengers connecting through the Gulf, Singapore or Europe to reach the wider world. That pattern is now shifting. IndiGo and the merged Air India group have both committed to building genuine long-haul networks of their own, and the early months of 2026 underline how quickly the competitive landscape is changing.

Two very different playbooks

Air India, now consolidated with Vistara under the Tata umbrella, is positioning itself as a full-service global carrier. Its strategy leans on a large order for Boeing and Airbus wide-bodies, a long-running cabin retrofit programme for its existing 777 and 787 fleet, and an expanding map of nonstop services from Delhi and Mumbai to North America and Europe.

IndiGo, historically a narrow-body low-cost specialist, is taking a more measured route. The airline has leaned on wet-leased and damp-leased wide-bodies to test long-haul demand while it awaits delivery of its own Airbus A350s later in the decade, allowing it to add Europe-bound services without committing prematurely to a large owned wide-body fleet.

Why hubs matter

The prize is connecting traffic. Both carriers want to capture passengers from India's interior and from neighbouring South Asian markets who currently transit foreign hubs. Strengthening Delhi and Bengaluru as origin points, and feeding them with dense domestic networks, is central to that ambition.

  • Air India is concentrating premium long-haul flying on Delhi and Mumbai.
  • IndiGo's unmatched domestic feed gives it a structural advantage on connectivity.
  • Bengaluru is emerging as a credible third international gateway.

Execution risk remains real, from pilot training pipelines to airport infrastructure constraints. But the strategic direction is clear: India increasingly intends to carry its own long-haul traffic rather than cede it to carriers headquartered elsewhere.

metaTitle: How IndiGo and Air India Are Reshaping India's Long-Haul Map
metaDescription: IndiGo and the merged Air India group are building long-haul networks from Delhi, Mumbai and Bengaluru, aiming to carry India's outbound traffic themselves.
